coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of weissella-derived fluorescence carbon dots for microbial staining, cell imaging and dual sensing of vitamin B12 and hexavalent chromium
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xiuling
Wang, Suping
Luo, Yiyong
Yuan, Xiaotian
Wei, Yunlin
Zhang, Qi
Qin, Kunhao
Tu, Yujiao
coredata.dc:description: Due to small size, strong fluorescence and low cytotoxicity, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) attract people's attention. The CDs-Ws derived from Weissella sp. Kl-3 with bright blue photoluminescent were obtained via hydrothermal method. The CDs-Ws could selectively label dead bacterial and yeast cells, and show the potential for the differentiation of live/dead microbe. It showed that the prepared CDs-Ws had multicolor fluorescence emission properties, excellent biocompatibility and low biotoxicity with cell and in vivo imaging. In addition, CDs-Ws sensitized by ampicillin sodium was employed as fluorescence nanosensor (CDs-Ws-Amp Na) to detect Cr(VI) and VB12 based on inner filter effet mechanism. The linear correction was obtained for VB12 detection in 0–25 μM with 51.50 nM detection limit, and the linear range was 0–50 μM with 106.57 nM detection limits for Cr(VI) by CDs-Ws-Amp Na. The fluorescent nanosensor was used for detection of Cr(VI) in water and VB12 in serum and milk. It shows excellent potential in bioimaging, Cr(VI) and VB12 detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bioimaging
Nanosensor
Detection
Fluorescence

Fluorescence quenching of Cr(Vl) and VB12
Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurement
Analysis in real water, serum and milk samples
Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and yeast
Cytotoxicity evaluation and cell bioimaging of CDs-Ws
“Turn-off” sensing strategy of CDs-Ws-Amp Na for Cr(VI) and VB12 detection
Sensing strategy for Cr(Vl) and VB12 detection
Possible quenching mechanisms of Cr(VI) and VB12 detection
Possible fluorescence sensitization mechanism by ampicillin sodium
Analysis in real water, serum and milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (inset: HR-TEM) (a), FTIR spectrum (b), XRD (c) and XPS (d) of CDs-Ws.
Images of live/dead bacteria (E. coli BW25113 and B. cereus 41–22) and two yeast (C. podzolicus and Rhodospordium sp. 25235) stained by CDs-Ws at CLSM.
HeLa cells imaging by CDs-Ws at CLSM.
Imaging of male BALB/c mice with CDs-Ws.
Fluorescence quenching selectivity of CDs-Ws towards Vitamins (a) and metal ions (b) at 1 mM concentration.

UV–Vis absorption of Cr(VI) (a), VB12 (b) and CDs-Ws-Amp Na fluorescence decay lifetime of CDs-Ws-Amp Na and CDs-Ws-Amp Na + Cr(VI) (c), +VB12 (d). UV–Vis absorption of Cr(VI) (e), VB12 (f) and Ex, Em of CDs-Ws-Amp Na.
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of Cr(VI) determination.
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of VB12 determination.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of weissella-derived fluorescence carbon dots for microbial staining, cell imaging and dual sensing of vitamin B12 and hexavalent chromium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Science and Technology
Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to small size, strong fluorescence and low cytotoxicity, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) attract people's attention. The CDs-Ws derived from Weissella sp. Kl-3 with bright blue photoluminescent were obtained via hydrothermal method. The CDs-Ws could selectively label dead bacterial and yeast cells, and show the potential for the differentiation of live/dead microbe. It showed that the prepared CDs-Ws had multicolor fluorescence emission properties, excellent biocompatibility and low biotoxicity with cell and in vivo imaging. In addition, CDs-Ws sensitized by ampicillin sodium was employed as fluorescence nanosensor (CDs-Ws-Amp Na) to detect Cr(VI) and VB12 based on inner filter effet mechanism. The linear correction was obtained for VB12 detection in 0–25 μM with 51.50 nM detection limit, and the linear range was 0–50 μM with 106.57 nM detection limits for Cr(VI) by CDs-Ws-Amp Na. The fluorescent nanosensor was used for detection of Cr(VI) in water and VB12 in serum and milk. It shows excellent potential in bioimaging, Cr(VI) and VB12 detection.

Schematic of the synthetic route for fluorescent CDs-Ws and the applications in bioimaging and Cr(VI), VB12 detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The traditional method for differentiating live and dead bacteria is mainly plate counting method, which is time-consuming and susceptible to culture conditions [1]. In recent years, instrumental and fluorescent staining methods have been developed [2]. The instrumentation consists mainly of atomic force microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and surface enhanced Raman scattering, which require a very large amount of time and money to be maintained, so they are not suitable for quickly differentiating live and dead bacteria. Fluorescence staining is one of the simple and rapid methods to evaluate bacterial activity [3,4]. As traditional commercial dyes, azidine and propidium iodide are by far the most commonly used fluorescent dye to stain dead bacterial DNA due to their penetration ability to the cell walls of dead bacteria. However, azidine and propidium iodide are usually toxic, light bleaching and expensive. Therefore, it is very important to exploit dyes with low or no toxicity, good light stability and low cost for bacterial detection.

Chromium is a heavy metal, and usually shows two kinds of states in aqueous, such as Cr(III) and Cr(VI) [5]. Due to its mobility and carcinogenicity, Cr(VI) is more toxic than Cr(III) in living organisms. Cr(VI) is mainly a chronic toxic to humans. It exhibits strong oxidizing effect, thus leading to chronic poisoning from local damage to irremediable progresses. Cr(VI) tolerable limit is 0.1 mg L−1 in drinking water by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [6].

Traditional approaches for Cr(VI) detection include atomic absorption spectrometry, ion chromatography, mass spectrometry, diphenylcarbazide (DPC) spectrophotometric method, X-ray imaging etc. These approaches exhibit accuracy and sensitivity, but they have some drawbacks: time-consuming, operation inconvenient and expensive [5]. Consequently, it is necessary to build a quick and easy sensor to monitor Cr(VI).

Due to small size, good biocompatibility, high fluorescence intensity and low cytotoxicity, fluorescent CDs have attracted people's attention [7]. CDs-based fluorescence nanosensor have been employed to determine pH, detect metal ions and pollution, drug delivery and bioimaging [8–11].

Vitamin B12 plays important roles in DNA synthesis, DNA repair, carbohydrate and enzyme metabolism, and nerve system maintenance [12]. But our body can not synthesize Vitamin B12, it can be obtained from animal-derived foods, such as egg, meat et al. For children, the lack of vitamin B12 can lead to mental disorders and anemia [13]. So, it is urgent to establish sensitivity methods to determine vitamin B12 in cell.

Weissella spp. are lactic acid bacteria, and mainly isolated from fermented foods, such as soy sauce, kimchi, tempeh, sausage, and play a crucial part in the synthesis of flavoring substances such as organic acids, esters and short-chain fatty acids in food. Some strains of Weissella spp. have the properties of synthesizing bacteriocins, oligosaccharides, exopolysaccharides and cellulose, which have potential probiotic properties. Weissella sp. Kl-3 was isolated from pickled radish of Kaili, Guizhou province. The isolates could produce lactic acid, and it is safe and non-toxic. In this paper, Weissella sp. Kl-3 derived CDs (CDs-Ws) were obtained via simple hydrothermal method. The CDs-Ws showed excellent potential in the differentiation of live/dead bacterial and yeast cells. In addition, ampicillin sodium can sensitize the fluorescence of CDs-Ws (turn-on), then the sensitized fluorescence was quenched by Cr(VI) and VB12 (turn-off). This is the first report on Weissella sp. Kl-3 derived CDs for two substances determination based on inner filter effect mechanism. Furthermore, the prepared CDs-Ws was employed to successfully detect Cr(VI) and VB12 in real water, serum and milk samples. There is no report on using Weissella sp. Kl-3 as starting material before.

All analytical reagents purchase from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. in China, including penicillin G sodium (Pen G Na), gentamicin sulfate (Gen), ampicillin sodium (Amp Na), amoxicillin (Amo), penicillin G potassium (Pen G K), cephalexin (Cep), tetracycline hydrochloride (Tet), ampicillin (Amp), Vitamin E (VE), Vitamin B6 (VB6), Vitamin H (VH), Vitamin B1 (VB1), Vitamin A (VA), Vitamin K3 (VK3), Vitamin B12 (VB12) and Vitamin C (VC).

Luria-Bertani (LB) media was used to incubate Escherichia coli and Bacillus cereus. Lactic acid bacteria fermentation medium MRS was used to incubate Weissella sp. Kl-3. YPD liquid media were used to incubate Cryptococcus podzolicus and Rhodospordium sp. 25235.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Weissella sp. Kl-3-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-Ws were obtained via simple hydrothermal method with Weissella sp. Kl-3 as material. The culture of Weissella sp. Kl-3 was incubated in MRS medium for 24 h at 37 °C with 150 rpm, collected, centrifuged (13,000 g, 10 min), dissolved in 30 mL deionized water, and then reaction at 200 °C for 6 h at high-pressure autoclave.
The CDs-Ws obtained via hydrothermal method were centrifuged (13,000 g, 10 min), filtrated by 0.22 μM filter membrane, and then dialyzed in water with dialysis membranes (500–1000 D, Acmec).

To study the differentiation of live/dead microbe with CDs-Ws, microbial cells, such as two types bacteria (E. coli, B. cereus) and two type yeast cells (C. podzolicus 52 and Rhodospordium sp. 25235) were chosen [10]. E. coli and B. cereus were incubated in LB liquid media (37 °C, 12 h), while C. podzolicus and Rhodospordium sp. 25235 was incubated in YPD medium (28 °C, 12 h). The dead cells were obtained by treating with 4% formaldehyde solution for 2 h [4]. All cells were observed under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

DMEM medium was used to culture HeLa cells. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MTT) assay assesses the cytotoxicity of HeLa cells with CDs-Ws. HeLa cells were starved for 1 h after 24 h cultivation and centrifuged. The medium were removed, and then washed with phosphate buffer. HeLa cells with different concentration CDs-Ws were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. All the samples fix with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min, and then cell imaging was observed under CLSM.

Bio-compatibility and bio-distribution of CDs-Ws was evaluated by male BALB/c mice about 20 g. The CDs-Ws (50 μg/mL) were intraperitoneal injected into anesthetized mice with ether, at the same time, the mice injected with 0.9% NaCl solution as control. The mice used are in accordance with the Association for Assessment and Accreditation of Laboratory Animal Care. IVIS Lumina Series III imaging system was used to image.

The 2 μL CDs-Ws and 998 μL phosphate buffer were mixed. Then, different concentration antibiotics solution assess the fluorescence sensitization of CDs-Ws. Fluorescence intensity was assayed at Ex 346 nm and Em 422 nm. To study the coexisting interference towards fluorescence intensity, ampicillin sodium and other substances was chosen, such as cephalexin, penicillin G potassium, penicillin G sodium, amoxicillin, gentamicin sulfate and tetracycline hydrochloride.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Cr(Vl) and VB12
ce:section.ce:para: The 2 μL CDs-Ws and 998 μL phosphate buffer were mixed, then different concentration Cr(Vl) and VB12 was added, and diluted with sterile water, then the fluorescence intensity was measured. The selectivity was studied to assay the interference of coexisting substances on the PL intensity.

Fluorescence sensitization rate follows the equation [14]:
%Sensitization=(F
s -F
0 )*100/F
0
where F
0 refers to CDs-Ws fluorescence intensity and F
s refers to CDs-Ws-Amp Na fluorescence intensity.

Fluorescence quenching rate follows the equation [14]:
%Quenching=(F
0 -F
Q )*100/F
0
where F
0 refers to CDs-Ws fluorescence intensity and F
Q refers to the CDs-Ws-Amp Na quenched fluorescence intensity after addition quenchers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as reference, and measured to be 54% at excited wavelength 360 nm. The QY equation is as follows [10]:
Q
CDs = Q
R (I
CDs /I
R ) (A
R /A
CDs ) (η
CDs
2/η
R
2)

Average lifetime model is as follows [10]:
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
( n = 1 , 2 , 3…… )

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis in real water, serum and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical feasibility of the CDs-Ws nanosensor was investigated in water samples. The water samples were collected, centrifuged, and then filtered with 0.22 μm filters membrane.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is employed to study the CDs-Ws morphology (Fig. 1 a). CDs-Ws had uniform spherical shapes about 2.2–8.7 nm particle size with average diameter 5.20 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is employed to analyze the CDs-Ws surface chemistry (Fig. 1b). A 3446 cm−1 strong absorption peak corresponding to N–H/O–H bonds [15]; a 1664 cm−1 peak corresponding to CC bonds; the 1403 cm−1 peak indicating the present of C–H/N–H/C–N bonds; the 1112 cm−1 peak indicates C–O/CS bonds [16]; and the 549 cm−1 peak reveals C–Cl bond. The XRD pattern of CDs-Ws exhibits a broad peak (2θ = 21.55°), revealing little crystalline nature (Fig. 1c).

Full scan XPS spectra with five peaks (285.24 eV, 531.70 eV, 399.87 eV, 163.34 eV and 133.02 eV) are corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and P2p, respectively (Fig. 1d). The elements atom ratios are 65.49%, 20.41%, 12.99%, 0.62% and 0.50%, respectively. Four peaks (284.05 eV, 284.70 eV, 285.90 eV and 287.85 eV) ascribes to assigns to CC, C–C/C–N, C–O and CO from C1s spectrum [17], (Fig. S1a). Two peaks (399.60 eV and 400.20 eV) spectrum attributes to N–H and C–H from N1s [20], respectively (Fig. S1b). Two peaks (530.90 eV and 532.2 eV) indicates CO and C–O from O1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) [17]. XPS and FTIR spectroscopy indicated the present of abundant hydrophilic groups and conferred good water solubility on CDs-Ws [18].
UV–Vis absorption spectrum is employed to feature the optical properties of CDs-Ws [19]. The absorption peak at 261 nm assigns to π-π* transitions (CC bond) [10,20].

The optimal excitation wavelength is 346 nm with maximum emission wavelength 424 nm (Fig. S2a). The fluorescence intensity increases and stays centered at round 430 nm when the excitation wavelength shifts (290–330 nm) [21]; the fluorescence intensity reduces when it shifts (340–400 nm), meanwhile the emission peak shows red-shift, therefore CDs-Ws show excellent excitation-dependent character (Fig. S2b).

Under different salt concentration, pH and storage time, CDs-Ws fluorescence stability was studied. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ws increases at pH 2.2–5.0 and then decreases (Fig. S3a), which demonstrates that CDs-Ws has optimal pH at 5.0. We observed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ws only slightly fluctuates with NaCl concentration increasing (0–1.0 mM) (Fig. S3b), and CDs-Ws is stable in 6 months (Fig. S3c).

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs-Ws determined to be 8.96%. The zeta average potential and fluorescence decay lifetime of CDs-Ws were −23.30 mV and 1.85 ns, respectively, revealing negatively charged groups presented on CDs-Ws surfaces [10,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and yeast
ce:section.ce:para: The bacterial and yeast staining was performed with the nanosensor of CDs-Ws. But we found that the CDs-Ws could not label the live bacterial and yeast cells (Fig. 2 ). Then the possibility of dead microbe were studied. The dead E. coli, B. cereus, C. podzolicus and Rhodospordium sp. 25235 were prepared by incubation with 4% formaldehyde solution (2 h). We found that the dead bacterial and yeast cells can be distinctly labeled with CDs-Ws (50 μg/mL).

The average value of zeta potential of CDs-Ws in water measured is −23.30 mV, which accords with the negatively charged nature of Weissella sp. Kl-3 cells (−30.47 mV). Zeta potential values of live E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41–22, C. podzolicus and Rhodospordium sp. 25235 measured are −43.63 mV, −40.00 mV, −36.03 mV and −32.40 mV, respectively. Therefore, due to strong electrostatic repulsion effect between live microbe and CDs-Ws, live microbial cells can not be labeled by CDs-Ws. Zeta potential values of dead E. coli BW25113, B. cereus 41–22, C. podzolicus and Rhodospordium sp. 25235 measured are −23.07 mV, −22.15 mV, −20.93 mV and −20.05 mV, respectively, after treatment with 4% formaldehyde solution. It indicated that the electrostatic interaction was greatly weakened between dead microbe and CDs-Ws. Selective permeability is ubiquitous in microbial cell membranes. Usually, ion channels transportation or trans-membrane proteins was required when small molecular substances entering cells, thus, CDs-Ws could not label live microbial cells. For dead microbial cells, the cell walls or membranes were destroyed, then selective permeability deduced, and the electrostatic interaction between microbe and CDs-Ws greatly weakened, therefore the dead cells could be successfully labeled by CDs-Ws.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and cell bioimaging of CDs-Ws
ce:section.ce:para: Bioimaging application potential of CDs-Ws was investigated and the cells cytotoxicity was assayed with MTT. HeLa cells viability was measured when cultivated with CDs-Ws, meanwhile, untreated cells as control. HeLa cells viability was higher than 85% even the CDs-Ws concentration reached 1500 μg/mL (Fig. S4). So, the prepared CDs-Ws showed a low cytotoxicity.

Bio-compatibility and mulcolor photoluminescence of CDs-Ws were investigated for bioimaging applications, and cell imaging was performed. Different colors fluorescence (green, blue and red) was observed in the nucleus of HeLa cells after incubation with CDs-Ws (50 μg/mL) (Fig. 3 ). It showed an excellent alternative for nuclear staining. In addition, there was no morphological change of HeLa cells by treating with CDs-Ws, confirming the low cytotoxicity of the CDs-Ws.

In vivo bio-distribution was used to investigate the CDs-Ws fluorescence penetration. We can not observe acute toxicological response, indicating the low cytotoxicity of CDs-Ws. Different bio-distribution images were obtained at 0 min, 5 min, 0.5 h, 1.5 h, 3 h, 4 h, 5 h and 6 h after injection. We could observe strong fluorescence signals in intestines, testicle, fur and stomach after 5 min (Fig. 4 ), but no fluorescence was observed in brain, liver, kidney and heart (Fig. S5). After 4 h, the fluorescence signal gradually declines, and disappears after 6 h. Thus, we inferred that CDs-Ws were absorbed by mice abdominal epidermis, then CDs-Ws entered into stomach, intestines and testicles, indicating targeted distribution, good biocompatibility, rapid excretion and low biotoxicity.

The fluorescence sensitization of different substances towards CDs-Ws was studied. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ws could be sensitized only by ampicillin sodium (Fig. S6), and denoted as CDs-Ws-Amp Na, therefore, Amp Na could be a “turn-on” nanosensor.

CDs-Ws-Amp Na shows an absorption peak at 262 nm, assigned to π–π* transitions. It has optimal excitation at 348 nm with maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm, slightly red-shifted than CDs-Ws (424 nm and 346 nm, respectively) (Figs. S7a and S7b). The fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ws-Amp Na increases when the excitation wavelength shifts (280–350 nm), and the fluorescence intensity reduces when it shifts (360–400 nm) (Fig. S8); meanwhile, the emission peak has a slight red-shift, showing excitation-dependent feature of CDs-Ws-Amp Na (Fig. S8). The CDs-Ws-Amp Na was stable in pH 5.0–12.0, the NaCl solution concentration (0–1.0 mM) and storage time within 8 months (Fig. S9), respectively.

The relation between the Amp Na concentration and CDs-Ws fluorescence sensitization efficiency was studied. With the increase of Amp Na concentrations, CDs-Ws fluorescence intensity increases (Fig. S7c). Good linear obtained between fluorescence quenching rate and the Amp Na at the concentration of 0–25 μM (R2 = 0.9985) with limit of detection (LOD) 13.87 nM (Fig. S7d). It was reported that CDs could be sensitized by Al(III) [23].

The particles of CDs-Ws-Amp Na occur aggregation by TEM (Fig. S10a). The surface chemistry of CDs-Ws-Amp Na was studied by FTIR, as showed in Fig. S10b. The XRD pattern of CDs-Ws-Amp Na exhibits a broad peak (2θ = 20.85°), revealing little crystalline nature (Fig. S10c).

Full scan XPS spectra with six peaks are corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, Na1s, S2p and P2p, respectively (Fig. S11a). Four peaks, two peaks and two peaks could be found from C1s, N1s and O1s spectra, respectively (Figs. S11b–S11d). XPS and FTIR spectroscopy indicated the abundant hydrophilic groups and conferred good water solubility on CDs-Ws-Amp Na.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn-off” sensing strategy of CDs-Ws-Amp Na for Cr(VI) and VB12 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CDs-Ws-Amp Na was studied. The possible interference includes metal ions and Vitamins. As shown in Fig. 5 a–b, VB12 and Cr(VI) can effectively quench CDs-Ws-Amp Na fluorescence. Thus, there is high selectivity between Cr(VI)/VB12 and CDs-Ws-Amp Na.

The effect of pH on the quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) and VB12 on CDs-Ws was studied. The fluorescent intensity increases and reaches the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency within pH 2.2–9.0, then the fluorescent intensity decreases, thus pH 9.0 is the optimum pH for Cr(VI) detection (Fig. S12a). The fluorescent intensity increases and reaches the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency within pH 2.2–4.0, then the fluorescent intensity decreases, therefore pH 4.0 is the optimum pH for VB12 detection (Fig. S12b). The effect of reaction time on Cr(VI) and VB12 detected is shown in Fig. S12c and Fig. S12d. The fluorescence quenching efficiency does not change significantly with the increase of reaction time. Therefore, 3 min and 2 min were chosen as the optimal reaction time for Cr(VI) and VB12 fluorescence response, respectively.

In order to investigate the interference with each other of Cr(VI) and VB12, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F)/F0 of Cr(VI) and VB12 towards CDs-Ws were measured at pH 9.0 and pH 4.0. It showed that the two types of test objects did not interfere with each other at different pHs (Fig. S13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy for Cr(Vl) and VB12 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI) and VB12 were used to study the sensing strategy for single substance detection. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ws-Amp Na could be quenched partly by Cr(VI) and VB12, respectively (Table S1). So, CDs-Ws-Amp Na can be employed as a fluorescence nanosnesor to detect Cr(VI) and VB12 [36]. Niu et al. reported that N-doped CDs could be quenched by Fe3+, and recovered with PPi, then quenched by ALP, therefore, the prepared N-doped CDs could be employed for PPi and ALP detection, respectively [37]. This is the first report on Weissella sp. Kl-3 derived CDs for Cr(VI) and VB12 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanisms of Cr(VI) and VB12 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the quenching process of quenchers on the fluorescence of CDs consist of five types, such as static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [38].
UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence decay lifetime were used to research the quenching process of Cr(VI) and VB12 to the fluorescence of CDs-Ws-Amp Na.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-Ws-Amp Na, Cr(VI)/VB12, CDs-Ws-Amp Na + Cr(VI)/VB12 are shown in Fig. 6 a–b. The CDs-Ws-Amp Na absorption remained unchanged with or without Cr(VI)/VB12, indicating no new substance. Therefore, SQ process did not work.

The fluorescence decay lifetime of CDs-Ws-Amp Na remained unchanged without (1.85 ns) or with Cr(VI) (1.83 ns) and VB12 (1.83 ns) (Fig. 6c–d). Therefore, we inferred that IFE worked in Cr(VI) and VB12 quenching process.
The CDs-Ws-Amp Na excitation spectrum overlapped the Cr(VI) and VB12 absorption spectrum (Fig. 6e–f), which verified the IFE process [38]. Thus, IFE may work in the quenching mechanism.

And IFE mechanism was further investigated by the Parker equation [38].
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

With the concentrations of Cr(VI) and VB12 increasing from 0 to 25 μM, F
cor /F
obsd values increased from 1.50 to 1.99, and from 1.50 to 2.73, respectively, matching the Parker equation. Therefore, the quenching processes of Cr(VI) and VB12 to CDs-Ws-Amp Na fluorescence all belonged to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence sensitization mechanism by ampicillin sodium
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-Ws fluorescence intensity was enhanced just because of ampicillin sodium, whereas other substances, even ampicillin, had negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity. We can find that whether hydroxyl group attached to Na is the only difference between ampicillin and ampicillin sodium. Thus, we deduced that CDs-Ws aggregate to larger particles when ampicillin sodium was added to the CDs-Ws, and it was verified by TEM of CDs-Ws-Amp Na (Fig. S10a), then cause the fluorescence signal intensity “turn-on” due to the strong affinity interactions between -Na of ampicillin sodium and the –S of CDs-Ws [39]. Therefore, with the increase of ampicillin sodium concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ws increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis in real water, serum and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-Ws nanosensor was used to determine Cr(VI) in water samples, and VB12 in serum and milk samples. The recoveries fell within 92.30–96.60% of water samples; the recoveries fell within 93.80–100.40% of serum samples; the recoveries fell within 94.90–97.60% of milk samples (Table S2). It is sensitive for CDs-Ws to detect Cr(VI) in water (n = 3), and VB12 in serum and milk samples (n = 3). Meanwhile, DPC spectrophotometric method was used to detect Cr(VI) in water samples, and high pressure liquid chromatography was used to determine VB12 in serum and milk samples (Table S2). Therefore, it is a new quick and easy way to detect Cr(VI) and VB12 in environment with CDs-Ws nanosensor.

Bright blue fluorescent CDs-Ws derived from Weissella sp. Kl-3 were obtained via simple hydrothermal synthesis method. CDs-Ws exhibits good fluorescence features. The CDs-Ws showed the ability of selectively differentiating live/dead bacterial and yeast cells. CDs-Ws showed good bio-compatibility, low bio-toxicity by cell and in vivo bioimaging. Furthermore, the CDs-Ws could be sensitized by ampicillin sodium (turn-on), and as fluorescence nanosensor (CDs-Ws-Amp Na) for Cr(VI) and VB12 determination. The good linear relationships were obtained between Cr(VI) and VB12 concentration and fluorescence quenching rate of CDs-Ws-Amp Na. IFE played in quenching the fluorescent of CDs-Ws-Amp Na by Cr(VI) and VB12. Thus, it shows a good application potential for bioimaging and dual sensing of Cr(VI) and VB12.

This work was financially funded by Young Talents Support Program of Yunnan Province (Ten Thousand People Plan) (YNWR-QNBJ-2019-202) and Science Research Foundation of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education of China (2020J0509).

Xiuling Ji: Methodology. Suping Wang: Data curation. Yiyong Luo: Methodology. Xiaotian Yuan: Data curation. Yunlin Wei: Investigation, Validation. Qi Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Kunhao Qin: Writing - review & editing. Yujiao Tu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108818.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Viability of soil bacteria: optimization of plate-counting technique and comparision between total counts and plate counts within different size groups
Microb Ecol
